Bread baking has a long history stretching back thousands of years. The earliest leavened breads were discovered by accident — wild yeast from the air landing in a wet grain mixture and causing it to ferment.

Modern home bakers have access to commercial yeast, which makes the process far more predictable than it was for ancient Egyptians. Still, variables like humidity, flour protein content, and water temperature affect the final loaf in ways that take time to understand.

Sourdough is a different beast entirely. The starter — a living culture of wild yeast and bacteria — needs regular feeding and attention. Some bakers treat their starters like pets, giving them names and worrying when they look sluggish after a cold spell.

I started baking bread seriously about three years ago, after watching a neighbor pull a perfect boule from a Dutch oven. The crust shattered when he tapped it, which he explained was the sign of good oven spring. I had no idea what that meant at the time.

Flour matters more than most recipes admit. Bread flour's higher gluten content gives structure, but all-purpose flour produces a softer crumb that many people prefer for sandwich loaves. The difference isn't huge, but it's noticeable once you've baked both back to back.
